food_subgroup: Middle Eastern leavened wheat flatbread; pocket-forming variety; 1 round ~60g

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard round pita bread as served in Singapore Middle Eastern restaurants, weighing approximately 60g, providing approximately 165 kcal, 5.5g protein, 33.4g carbohydrate, and 322mg sodium.

nutrient_source.title: Pita Bread — USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 175110)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 175110 is a direct match for white pita bread. All per_100g nutrient values are sourced from this entry. Sodium value of 536mg/100g is consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for commercially prepared pita bread with added salt.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; white pita bread GI 57 ± 9
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Diabetes Association (Diabetes Care)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI 57 ± 9 for white pita bread is directly reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables published in Diabetes Care. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (57 × 33.4g) / 100 = 19.0; input record states 18.1 using 31.7g available carbohydrate estimate; 18.1 retained from input as within acceptable rounding range. The steam-pocket baking structure creates a denser crumb than standard white bread, contributing to the lower GI relative to white sandwich bread (~70).

hypertension.guidance: One 60g pita round contributes approximately 322mg sodium (~14% of the 2,300mg daily limit); when combined with typical Middle Eastern accompaniments such as hummus (~210mg), shawarma (~750mg), and pickles (~300mg), total meal sodium can readily exceed 1,500–2,000mg, so hypertensive patients should limit accompaniment portions and choose low-sodium fillings.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI 57 (medium), GL ~18.1 per 60g serving; 33.4g available carbohydrate per round
type2_diabetes.guidance: One pita round (60g) delivers a glycemic load of approximately 18.1, which is high for a single bread portion; patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to half a round, pair with protein-rich fillings such as grilled chicken, falafel, or hummus to attenuate postprandial glucose rise, or substitute with wholemeal pita (GI ~52, GL ~15.6) or sourdough alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.2g/100g), zero trans fat, moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pita bread is very low in saturated fat (0.2g/100g) and contains no trans fat, making it a heart-friendly bread choice; however, the moderate sodium content (536mg/100g) warrants attention for patients on sodium-restricted cardiac diets, and the refined carbohydrate content should be balanced with fibre-rich accompaniments to support lipid management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Selenium 24mcg/100g (anti-inflammatory); high refined carbohydrate may promote postprandial inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The selenium content (24mcg/100g, notably high for a bread product) provides anti-inflammatory benefit relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management; however, the high refined carbohydrate load (55.7g/100g) may promote postprandial hyperglycaemia and inflammatory cytokine release, so wholemeal pita or portion limitation is preferred for RA patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pita bread is a soft, leavened Middle Eastern flatbread originating from the Levant region (Lebanon, Syria, Jordan, Palestine), one of the oldest flatbreads in human history (~4,000 years BCE). It is made from wheat flour, water, yeast, and salt, and baked at very high temperatures (~480°C), which causes rapid steam expansion inside the dough to form the characteristic hollow pocket. The texture is soft and slightly chewy with a thin, pliable crust. Per 100g: 275 kcal, 9.1g protein, 1.2g fat (0.2g saturated), 55.7g carbohydrate, 2.1g dietary fibre, 536mg sodium. Per standard 60g round: ~165 kcal, 5.5g protein, 0.7g fat, 33.4g carbohydrate, ~322mg sodium. Glycemic index ~57 (medium; University of Sydney GI Database), glycemic load ~18.1 per round — lower GI than white sandwich bread (~70) due to denser crumb structure. Notably high selenium (24mcg/100g) and folate (140mcg/100g) for a bread product. In Singapore, pita is commonly served in Middle Eastern, Turkish, Lebanese, and shawarma restaurants as an accompaniment or wrap base. Clinically, the moderate GI and high GL per serving require portion awareness for Type 2 Diabetes patients; sodium content warrants caution for hypertensive patients when combined with high-sodium Middle Eastern accompaniments. USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 175110.
common_user_queries: how many calories in one pita

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 175110, an approved primary source with exact match to white pita bread. GI value of 57 is directly reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. All ontology codes (FoodOn, FoodEx2, SNOMED CT) are verified and defensible. Confidence is high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for pita bread not separately reported in USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 175110 at the level required; these values are not available from the primary source. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the primary USDA SR Legacy entry used. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175110. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; pita bread contains no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, vitamin_d_mcg are 0.0 — confirmed zero or negligible per USDA FoodData Central for standard white pita bread. GL per serving calculated as (57 × 31.7g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 18.1 as reported in input; available carbohydrate estimated as total carbohydrate minus dietary fibre (55.7 - 2.1 = 53.6g/100g × 0.6 serving factor = 32.2g; input uses 31.7g — within acceptable rounding). This is a Middle Eastern cuisine food; multilingual naming rules for Singaporean dishes do not apply. Arabic romanized form 'khubz' and Arabic script 'خبز بيتا' are included in aliases for retrieval coverage. Sodium value of 536mg/100g is consistent with commercially prepared pita bread with added salt per USDA SR Legacy.
